title: Marc to Market
content:
For centuries, people have bought art because they want to possess it, live with it, and control it. However, the Metropolitan Opera is forcing the art world to rethink one of its most fundamental assumptions about ownership. The New York institution is selling two massive paintings by Marc Chagall that have defined the opera house lobby since 1966. Titled The Sources of Music and The Triumph of Music, they dominate the Met’s narrow lobby and Lincoln Center. The two murals are deeply linked to their specific location, featuring famous composers flying over Manhattan.

If they were to move anywhere else, they would lose the idiosyncratic charm that makes them so incredibly desirable. That is why the Met’s general manager sees no problem imposing a major condition on their sale: they must remain in situ. He argues that the image of the Chagalls inside the facade of the Met is an iconic symbol of New York. As a result, their true value for any prospective buyer lies entirely in keeping them at the opera house. This unprecedented sale raises fascinating questions about the very nature of art ownership and location-bound value.

Some compare this proposed deal to naming rights, immovable ancient stone money, or even modern digital tokens. The most direct parallel is the sale of the Charging Bull statue on Wall Street, whose value comes from its rootedness. However, the Chagall murals are a much bigger deal, literally and financially, with a staggering $55 million price tag. Sheer financial desperation from the Met's operational budget shortfall is driving this highly unusual market experiment. If the Chagalls do sell under these conditions, they will immediately set a hugely important precedent for the art world.
